meta.title: Analyse ideas and values of the classical world
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves analysing ideas and values of the classical world.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Undertakes a methodical examination and interpretation of selected ideas and values in a classical literary text.

positive_signals: Structured explanation interpreting an idea/value within textual context
Analysis of how the idea/value is conveyed through language or events
Connection of the idea/value to broader themes in the text
Use of specific textual references to illustrate interpretation
negative_signals: Mere plot summary without interpretive analysis
Listing ideas/values without explaining their significance
Vague generalizations about the text's content
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Interpretation involves explaining the meaning or implications of ideas/values, not just describing the storyline.

success_examples: provided a simple response to the question
treated the characters in fictional texts as though they were real

omis.description: Includes relevant primary‐source evidence (quoted or referenced) to support analysis.

positive_signals: Direct quotation from the classical text
Specific reference to a passage in the primary source
Citation linked directly to the analytical point
Explanation of how the evidence supports the argument
negative_signals: Vague references without specific quotes
Use of secondary sources or summaries instead of primary
Quoting without relating back to the analysis
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Primary-source evidence must be from the original text itself, not from external summaries or commentaries.

success_examples: provided narrow examples from the text
occasionally generalising aspects of the plot

omis.description: Discusses the identified ideas and values in relation to the text.

positive_signals: Explanation of how an idea/value functions in the text
Discussion of the role of ideas/values in character development or plot
Analysis of textual elements illustrating the idea/value
Connection between the idea/value and specific scenes or dialogues
negative_signals: Simply listing ideas/values without discussion
Describing plot events without linking to ideas/values
Generic statements not tied to the text
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Discussion requires explaining the relationship between ideas/values and the text, not just identifying them.

success_examples: partially referenced ideas and / or values of the classical world

omis.description: Draws basic conclusions about the ideas and values analysed.

positive_signals: Explicit statement summarizing the analysis
Inference drawn from the evidence presented
Clear takeaway about the ideas/values in the text
Link between analysis and overall conclusion
negative_signals: Restating facts without drawing conclusions
Vague or incomplete summaries
Conclusions not supported by the preceding analysis
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Conclusions must synthesize the analysis into a new insight, not just repeat the evidence.

omis.description: Responds directly to the chosen examination question and its stated focus.

positive_signals: Direct reference to the question's focus (e.g., leadership, identity)
Analysis tailored to the specific prompt
Consistent connection between response and question requirements
Addressing all aspects of the chosen question
negative_signals: Generic essay ignoring the specific focus
Tangential discussion not related to the question
Choosing a question but not addressing its core elements
evidence_guide.common_confusion: The response must align with the exact focus of the selected question, not a general discussion of the text.

success_examples: responded to the question selected, though it may not have been the most suitable
Candidates are encouraged to choose the appropriate question for their text

omis.description: Explores more than one viewpoint or interpretation of the ideas and values.

positive_signals: Presentation of at least two contrasting perspectives
Discussion of alternative interpretations of the same idea/value
Comparison of different viewpoints within the text
Acknowledgment of interpretive possibilities
negative_signals: Presenting only a single viewpoint
Listing multiple ideas without contrasting them
Superficial mention of alternatives without exploration
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Exploration requires discussing and comparing viewpoints, not just mentioning them in passing.

success_examples: answered all aspects of the question, though their responses may have been unbalanced

omis.description: Makes explicit comparisons (e.g., between characters, themes, or cultural contexts).

positive_signals: Use of contrastive language like 'whereas' or 'in contrast'
Direct comparison between two or more elements
Analysis of similarities and differences
Explicit linkage between compared elements
negative_signals: Listing elements side-by-side without comparison
Describing elements separately without contrast
Implied comparisons without explicit language
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comparison requires explicit contrast or similarity statements, not just juxtaposition.

success_examples: included a range of examples which were described and analysed with depth

omis.description: Analyses a range of specific, relevant examples from across the text.

positive_signals: Examples from multiple sections or episodes of the text
Detailed analysis of each example's relevance
Coverage of different parts of the text
Integration of examples into the overall argument
negative_signals: Examples clustered in one part of the text
Quoting without analysis
Irrelevant or superficial examples
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Examples must be analyzed for their significance, not just listed or quoted.

success_examples: showed in-depth knowledge of the text and / or awareness of section(s) of the text

omis.description: Draws developed conclusions that are explicitly supported by primary-source evidence.

positive_signals: Conclusions citing specific primary evidence
Explanation of how evidence supports the conclusion
Developed inferences beyond basic summaries
Clear linkage between evidence and conclusion
negative_signals: Unsupported claims or generalizations
Conclusions without reference to evidence
Weak or indirect connections to evidence
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Conclusions must be explicitly tied to primary-source evidence, not assumed or implied.

omis.description: Integrates socio-historical context to explain how it influenced the text’s creation and ideas.

positive_signals: Context woven into analysis of specific examples
Explanation of how historical factors shaped the text
Linkage between socio-historical elements and ideas/values
Integration throughout the response, not isolated
negative_signals: Generic historical background without analysis
Context mentioned but not connected to the text
Tacked-on information at the end
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Context must be integrated into the analysis, explaining its influence on the text's creation and content.

success_examples: linked well to the wider historical / political / social context
clear understanding of both the ideas and values seen in the Ancient Greek world of Aristophanes

omis.description: Provides critical evaluation, establishing textual connections and discussing wider implications of the analysis.

positive_signals: Evaluative language showing insight (e.g., significance, impact)
Explicit connections between different parts of the text
Discussion of broader implications beyond the text
Critical assessment of ideas/values
negative_signals: Summarizing without evaluative insight
Isolated analysis without connections
Lack of discussion on wider implications
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Critical evaluation requires insightful judgment and connections, not just description.

success_examples: the question is frequently referred to, and the keywords ‘enhance’ or ‘diminish’ are used throughout
